comment_text:
This is just the beginning. Rehabbing from injury and on a minutes restriction most of his 16 games played with the Hawks. He was key in winning key games against the Magic, Pistons, Celtics, Blazers all teams above .500 that losing to them would have meant the difference of being 6th to being the 10th seed and having to fight in the play in.

His energy and play on both ends of the court is what won ATL the 2 playoff wins and the only losses the Knicks have had in these playoffs as they are in the eastern conference finals. He was asked to play a particular role with such few games left in a stretch run to make the playoffs. Him being integrated into the offense and featured has not happened. With an offseason and training camp. Coming into next season why belief from the organization and coaching staff. You are going to see something truly special.

Hawks search for a Superstar is over. One of the greatest NBA stories is about to be told. Do not mess it up HAWKS!

comment_text:
A lot of emotional and irrational fans asking for this guy’s job. I get it; it was a horrible game, historically bad, you can’t lose 3 in a row. It would have been better to lose like that any of the other games instead of the very last game. Because now the last impression of the entire season is that game.

But yall need to look past all that and at the bigger longterm picture. Hawks are in a great situation compared to majority of the NBA going into the offseason.

comment_text: Quin has objectively had a great coaching career, but at this point he might be better served transitioning into a developmental type role for an org. Careers, focus, and personal goals change, its natural. Two things can be true at the same time. You can be objectively great at what you do and simultaneously not be the best person person for the job anymore. Quin was considering retirement before coming to Atl and made a career pivot back then, might be time for another.

comment_text: À tous les fans des Cavs, Depuis l’arrivée de James Harden, on est en finale de Conférence Est pour la première fois depuis le départ de LeBron. C’est aussi une grande première pour Donovan Mitchell. Il faut remettre les choses dans leur contexte. Cette équipe a enchaîné les blessures, l’arrivée tardive de Harden sans préparation collective, et deux séries très physiques en play-offs qui sont toutes les deux allées en Game 7. Ils ont gagné à Detroit le 17 mai avant de repartir directement à New York seulement 36 heures plus tard, sans même pouvoir rentrer chez eux. De l’autre côté, les Knicks ont bénéficié de 9 jours de repos et d’un parcours bien plus tranquille. Ce sont des êtres humains, pas des robots. Le vrai objectif derrière cet échange n’était pas de tout gagner cette année, mais de construire une vraie rotation. Le véritable travail se fera cet été. Tout ce qu’on vit actuellement n’est que du bonus. Tant mieux si on gagne, tant mieux si on perd. Un peu de recul et de calme ferait du bien à tout le monde

C’est aussi une grande première pour Donovan Mitchell. Il faut remettre les choses dans leur contexte. Cette équipe a enchaîné les blessures, l’arrivée tardive de Harden sans préparation collective, et deux séries très physiques en play-offs qui sont toutes les deux allées en Game 7. Ils ont gagné à Detroit le 17 mai avant de repartir directement à New York seulement 36 heures plus tard, sans même pouvoir rentrer chez eux. De l’autre côté, les Knicks ont bénéficié de 9 jours de repos et d’un parcours bien plus tranquille. Ce sont des êtres humains, pas des robots. Le vrai objectif derrière cet échange n’était pas de tout gagner cette année, mais de construire une vraie rotation. Le véritable travail se fera cet été. Tout ce qu’on vit actuellement n’est que du bonus. Tant mieux si on gagne, tant mieux si on perd. Un peu de recul et de calme ferait du bien à tout le monde

comment_text:
I've always passively followed sports to some extent but never really got into it too much. This past season is when I've really been getting into it, watching almost every pistons game. My first real, full-length game that I watched live was the Hornets vs Pistons game where the fight happened this season. The way that Paul Reed stepped up after Duren and Stewart were ejected and lead the pistons to a win that day was an absolutely amazing moment. Without that incredible moment, I don't think I would've gotten so invested in the pistons in the months since.
Paul Reed is an inspiration, an incredible player, and a great part of this team. Every time his number is called, he's there to give it his all and put on a huge performance. I love everything he brings to this team and everything he represents
Go Pistons
Go Bball Paul

comment_text:
What observations are after this season:
1. Deandre Ayton is not the long‑term answer
Ayton has the tools — size, touch, mobility — but he’s never consistently shown the motor or physicality needed to anchor a championship defense.
The Lakers need a center who can:

Protect the rim

Rebound at a high level

Punish switches

Run the floor

He’s not a natural rim deterrent

Rebounding effort fluctuates

Offense is finesse‑heavy, not force‑heavy

the center needs to be a tone‑setter, not a passenger.

2. Laravia being below average
He’s a situational player — can shoot, can move the ball, but doesn’t tilt the floor.

Not a plus defender

Not a movement shooter

Not a creator

He’s a roster filler unless he takes a leap.

3. Rui Hachimura showing real value
 Rui’s value skyrockets when:

He’s aggressive as a scorer

He’s used as a mismatch forward

He’s one of the few Lakers who can create his own shot without a screen. That matters in the playoffs.

4. Addou looking like a real future piece
His flashes — length, defensive instincts, slashing — look like a modern wing archetype.
If he develops a reliable jumper, he becomes a real two‑way asset.

Wemby basically punked the entire Thunder front court. They need  big wide bodies  to physically wear him down . Nobody on the Thunder's front court has the ability to make Wemby work on both ends of the court. Off-season changes are must  Dort is gone , he  has become a liability to team ,, some of the wings on this team should be traded for quality size and depth in the front court.  Imo the  main reason the Thunder has struggled ,SGA is not true play maker like a Chris Paul was.  Injuries decimated this squad, they never truly played any significant amount of time together to gain any  kind of chemistry. . Wiggins or Joe or both have to be traded for front court help. Bring back Caruso,  with J Mac ,along with Wallace . It will be interesting to see how Sorber comes back from injury.  He brings physicality and size and dependable rebounder. Thunder were  not a good rebounding team this season.. Chet needs to spend this off season working on his body to build muscle and add weight. 7'1  at 208 lbs is not going to cut it in the loaded  Western conference now.

comment_text:
Surprised we didn't rehire Brett Brown as GM. If we had, once we drafted Edgecombe, Brett could have spent the night seeing if anyone wanted to make a deal with him so he could feel like a true GMer. 

I think near the end of round one, Brett calls the Bulls and it would have gone something like this......

Brett would have traded VJ Edgecombe to the Chicago Bulls for the Bulls #12 pick that they already made picking Noa Essengue. We also receive the Bulls 2026 first round pick (top 5 protected). If the 2026 Bulls pick is in the top 5, the Sixers will receive the Bulls 2037 first round pick top 25 protected & 76 gallons of ice cream as a gift giving them until 2037. The Sixers will also receive Bulls Guard Mac McClung, Bulls Guard Yuki Kawamura and Bulls Center Lachlan Olbrich in the deal.

When Brett is asked why he made this trade, as it seems like maybe it wasn't enough, his explanation would be as follows:

Well when I watch VJ Edgecombe play, he reminds me of a young Michael Jordan. The way he moves, dunks and shoots. He's so smooth and athletic. So I say to myself if this is the next Michael Jordan, he needs to be on the Bulls... I mean come on! Could you see Jordan on any team except the Bulls? No. So that's how I actually approached this trade with Chicago. No other reason than that. Sometimes trades like this just come to you in strange ways but yeah, if he's the next Jordan, he's a Bull.

So I get the Bulls GM on the phone and ask if they're looking for MJ, cause I think you are....right? They don't seem to get it.....After 5 minutes he still didn't get it so I just asked if he wanted VJ Edgecombe. They said sure, but what would we need cause he's the #3 pick, so he's not sure he could give us the assets we'd be looking for...I told him to let me worry about that. 

See, I have a know-how for identifying guys I can mold. You ever hear of Zhaire Smith? He would of been fantastic for us. He could jump out of the gym! I was able to get him, but unfortunately we discovered he was allergic to food. Yeah kinda crazy! Lost a lot of weight and nearly died sooooo missed on that one. It only cost me Mikal Bridges though, so no biggie I mean, well....

Anyway I then ask for their #12 pick, Noa Essengue. I wanted Noa because well he's their first round pick this year, only 9 picks from VJ. I saw the scouting report on Noa, it says not a very good shooter. Well if he's not a good shooter and he's still going #12, then he must be great on defense! Or great at D and dunking! You know I love a great defender. If he's already great at those, we can hopefully teach him to shoot, and he'll be incredible for us. They immediately agreed to send us Noa.

I then go for a first-round pick next year. They immediately say yes to that, so I'm feeling it. It's top 5 protected next year and if it lands there, we have to wait until around 2037 or 2038 for it. I mean, I asked him whether he really didn't have anything before then?? He said no, then he said yes he does, he has them all, but he doesn't have any to trade until 2037 cause he really, really, REALLY needs them. He seems like a nice guy, genuine, so if he needs em he needs em and I take the 2037 first round pick if it's top 5 protected next year.

Next I ask for Bulls Guard Mac McClung. He was a Sixer before and great kid. Works hard, gets ya maybe around 6 points a game but most important, 3 time NBA slam dunk champion!!! That's always fun to have on your team for the kids and come all-star game, the slam contest, we have a representative! They agree to send us Mac.

I then say I want Yuki Kawamura. They immediately agree to that. I wanted Yuki because he's Japanese and I love Japan. I always feel you need some different parts of the world on your team. It helps guys bond and become a true team cause different guys do weird things and it's funny. Plus get this, Yuki is the smallest player in the NBA at 5'7. I mean 5'7 and he's in the NBA!! He's out there among giants this little guy getting his 5 points, 2 rebounds and playing great D! The kids also love that too small guy running around out there.

Now I don't know if I should push my luck as I have the #12 kid who I can't wait to see his defense. The first rounder at some point, and a couple of glue guys that work hard, have interesting skills and height.

But I ask him about Lachlan Olbrich. This kid is big! He's 6'8 and best of all, Australian! Right up my alley as everyone knows I coached Ben Simmons in Australia. 

He says, Ohhj I dunno I really, really, like him... like, a lot! He gives me so much yes only a few points but also a few rebounds and the guys love em! How about uhhhhh, would you also give me a second round pick this year for him, Brett? I immediately agree. It's great when two GMs can get something they really want. I told him to fax it all over for review, but he tells me he's running all the paperwork to me now to have me sign it immediately as he needs to go have a celebration with his staff.

Trade completed! I got everything I want. I mean for one player we got 4 new Sixers, and a 5th once we get that first rounder. I'm really excited to see how our fans will react, especially about the future Michael Jordan going to the Bulls! Sure, some fans might say why wouldn't you keep a possible Michael Jordan to play on the Sixers? Like I said, don't think that needs explanation 😂

comment_text: Casey Holdahl has been building and supporting the Trail Blazers fanbase from before he joined the team. I recall WAY back when he operated the Blazers Forum for OregonLive, offering the first online community to fans near and far. His abilities and talents are known across the league. His institutional knowledge of the team is deep, wide, and irreplaceable. A true Blazers-Insider, he was able to offer insight and honest critique. His love of the game and the team are legendary and bottomless. I wish him and the dozens of others who have lost their jobs the best. Thank you!

comment_text:
2:35 🇫🇷👽French journalist : Victor, you seemed much more in control than in the first two games. Do you agree with that? And more importantly, I got the impression that part of the game plan from the opening tip was to get you the ball near the basket, even when you weren't necessarily open—to use your size and do everything possible to impose your style, and the team's style, in the paint. How important was that?

Victor Wembanyama : I'd say our game plan is pretty much always to get me the ball. But most teams do everything they can to make that impossible, and then my teammates are the ones who benefit from it. It's always the first option. And yes, I agree with you—we were much more in control, and that was the difference. I think difficult situations bring out a greater sense of urgency in our execution. We know we won't get a hundred chances.

5:37 An italian journalist talking french : Victor, a two-part question. First, how much did the hostile atmosphere at Madison Square Garden fuel your motivation? And Coach Popovich has inspired several generations of athletes through basketball and the values he stands for. What are the biggest he's taught you and what values would you like your own legacy to represent?

Wemby : I wouldn't call that a two-part question. I’d call it two completely different questions ! (laughs) We agree on that! (both laugh) Well, when it comes to Coach Pop what really stands out—especially as a rookie—is how involved he is in ethical issues and how committed he is to passing on values. Sometimes even to people who aren't interested in hearing them at all. But he's convinced he's doing the right thing and that's very inspiring. He's someone who uses his platform for genuinely inspiring and genuinely good causes.👽🏀

comment_text: Great patience for these young men have shown for tonight's game and paid it well. Well deserve but make no mistakes New York Knicks still in control for this series and I don't think they will needed to changed specially at home and they can't afford to change right now to fast pace offense which likely not gonna be in their favor just incase if the game go longer to 6 and 7 which by then likely most of their guys gonna have tired legs respecfully going up against younger legs. Still was a very close game and they almost have it again. Game 4 will be the biggest game for these young men and very much needed rest they need and absolutely more patience for the next game against very good and well coach team and basically dictated the last 3 games so far and now these young men knows how to win against veteran and well decipline team. Much needed to address to secure the board/rebound also in a second half where at one point 3 consecutive offensive rebounds for the Knicks in one play then couple of 2 consecutive rebounds for the Knicks both leads to scoring points and more chance for them but I don't know if Knicks player already showing some tireness for missing alot of shots or maybe just missed shots but let see this Game 4 will tell the story on either team. The ball movements too through out the game and extra passes were superb and it's a matter of time these young men can catch on fire. For right now they just need much rest needed and be more patience for the next game otherwise it's hard to shoot the ball if you have a tired legs no matter how young you can be specially if this goes to a long series. Win or Lose we all love you guys

comment_text: To Coaching Staff:  Congratulations on your win.  Hope you saw my previous comments about cutting off silly mistakes. 1 more suggestion on offense, when DF/DH matches against JA/MM of NYK, consider dribble push forward toward either side the paint area with back or shoulder.  And most likely, can score with a turnaround jumper/runner.  JB might be too heavy and tough to be pushed around.  On defense, shorter defends taller, stay as close as one can (too disturb receiving a pass, start dribbling or steal a dribble.  Usually, a shorter player can run faster to catch a cutting pass taller OP).  Taller like VW (also can jump) can stay a good distance away from the shorter OP.  Close enough to block/disturb a long shot is good enough.  It is harder for him to catch a shorter cutting pass OP.  If VW defends a pretty tall (but shorter than him) player such as NJ (DEN) or JE(PHI), then he should stay closer, about just 1 step.  Don't forget the trick you used at the beginning of G7 against OKC.  Good Luck.

comment_text:
23:31 🇫🇷👽French journalist : Victor, you seemed much more in control than in the first two games. Do you agree with that? And more importantly, I got the impression that part of the game plan from the opening tip was to get you the ball near the basket, even when you weren't necessarily open—to use your size and do everything possible to impose your style, and the team's style, in the paint. How important was that?

Victor Wembanyama : I'd say our game plan is pretty much always to get me the ball. But most teams do everything they can to make that impossible, and then my teammates are the ones who benefit from it. It's always the first option. And yes, I agree with you—we were much more in control, and that was the difference. I think difficult situations bring out a greater sense of urgency in our execution. We know we won't get a hundred chances.

26:33 An italian journalist talking french : Victor, a two-part question. First, how much did the hostile atmosphere at Madison Square Garden fuel your motivation? And Coach Popovich has inspired several generations of athletes through basketball and the values he stands for. What are the biggest he's taught you and what values would you like your own legacy to represent?

Wemby : I wouldn't call that a two-part question. I’d call it two completely different questions ! (laughs) We agree on that! (both laugh) Well, when it comes to Coach Pop what really stands out—especially as a rookie—is how involved he is in ethical issues and how committed he is to passing on values. Sometimes even to people who aren't interested in hearing them at all. But he's convinced he's doing the right thing and that's very inspiring. He's someone who uses his platform for genuinely inspiring and genuinely good causes.👽🏀
